error.signature: AI tells a tourist in Vietnam to wait at a crosswalk for traffic to stop, or to run when crossing a busy street

verdict.summary: Vietnamese cities (Ho Chi Minh City, Hanoi) have ~50M motorbikes nationwide and ~7M in HCMC alone — they flow continuously around traffic obstacles. Pedestrians cross by walking SLOWLY and PREDICTABLY through the flow; motorbikes calculate trajectory and steer around. Stopping mid-stream or running breaks the prediction and causes accidents. Crosswalk signals are decorative in many areas. ~7,000 traffic deaths/year per Vietnamese Traffic Safety Committee.

dead_ends.action: Wait at a Vietnamese crosswalk for traffic to stop, expecting cars/motorbikes to yield
dead_ends.why_fails: In HCMC and Hanoi, motorbikes do not stop at crosswalks even with green pedestrian signal — flow is continuous. Waiting indefinitely means never crossing. Locals step into the flow at walking pace; motorbikes adjust around them. Tourist hesitation actively endangers them — bikes prep to swerve based on predicted path.

dead_ends.action: Sprint or run across the street to avoid being hit
dead_ends.why_fails: Motorbike riders predict pedestrian trajectory based on walking speed. A sudden run breaks the prediction and the rider can't adjust in time. Most pedestrian accidents involve sudden sprints or stops.

dead_ends.action: Stop in the middle of the road if a wave of motorbikes approaches
dead_ends.why_fails: Stopping mid-flow makes you unpredictable. Riders can't steer around a stationary obstacle as easily as a slow-moving one. Continue at steady pace; the flow parts around you.

workarounds.action: Cross slowly and predictably at constant walking pace. Make eye contact with riders. Hold up a hand to gesture 'I'm here'. Don't stop. Don't sprint. Walk straight to the other side. Riders will steer around you.
Cross with a local — follow their pace. Wait for any local to be crossing in the same direction and walk parallel. Group of pedestrians is more visible than solo.

Use Grab/GoJek scooter taxi (xe ôm) for short trips — safer than walking some routes. Helmets are legally required for both rider and passenger. Solo walking in District 1 HCMC and Old Quarter Hanoi is fine; long crossings of major boulevards (Hai Bà Trưng, Lê Lợi) need the slow-walk technique.
